content_html:
INF10025 Data Management and AnalyticsLecture 7 – Part 3
Group By / Having
MS Access & SQL

SQL Group By
The Group By clause must always be used in conjunction with an aggregate expressionwhenever you want a non aggregate expressionin the select clause
Add a non-aggregate expression to both
The Select clause
The Group By clause
Add an aggregate expression only to the:
The Select clause
SELECT	 relyear, COUNT(*)
FROM 	 movie
GROUP BY 	 relyear
Aggregate Expression
Non Aggregate Expression

SQL Group By
We want count how many movies exist for each rating code
Add the column rating_code (non-aggregate expression) to both:
The Select clause
The Group By clause
Add the COUNT(*) (aggregate expression) to only the:
The Select clause
SELECT	 rating_code, COUNT(*)
FROM 	 movie
GROUP BY 	 rating_code

SQL Group By
We want to list the average running lengthof movies for each year
Add the column relyear (non-aggregate expression) to both:
The Select clause
The Group By clause
Add the AVG(runtime) (aggregate expression) toonly the:
The Select clause
Notice how the result set is displayed in random year sequence. Not user friendly.
SELECT	relyear, AVG(runtime)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear

SQL Group By
The ORDER BY clause causes rows in the result set to be sequenced by a specified column.
SELECT	relyear, AVG(runtime)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear
ORDER BY   	relyear DESC
SELECT	relyear, AVG(runtime)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear
ORDER BY   	AVG(runtime)  ASC

SQL Group By
Using the Column Number in the Order By clause
SELECT	relyear,  COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear
ORDER BY   	2 DESC
The 2 refers to the 2nd column of the result set.
So the rows appear in descending count(*) sequence

SQL Group By
Consider this result set
It shows a count of movies by year
Within each year
It shows a count of movies by rating_code
So we can see that in 2013:
There were 4 'M' movies
There were 6 'PG' movies
This result is similar to the similar to previous group by results.
However this example has
two non-aggregate expressions
one aggregate expression

SQL Group By with multiple   non-aggregate columns
We want the count of each rating type in each year
Add relyear, rating_code (non-aggregate expressions) to both:
The Select clause
The Group By clause
Add COUNT(*) (aggregate expression) to ONLY the:
The Select clause
SELECT	relyear, rating_code, COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear, rating_code
ORDER BY 	relyear,  rating_code

SQL Group By
Consider this SQL statement
The result set lists the number of movies per year
Many of the years have a small number of movies
Suppose we are only interested in only years that have a total of 5 or more movies
SELECT	relyear, COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear

SQL Group By
SELECT	relyear, COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear
So we only want to keep rows that have aCOUNT(*) value >= 5 in the result set
We want to keep the rows that are not crossed out

SQL Group By Having
The Having clause only keeps a row in the result setif a value that meets a specified criteria
Usage:
Copy the aggregate expression in the Select clause and place it in the Having clause
Count(*)
SELECT	relyear, COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear
HAVING	COUNT(*) >= 5

SQL Group By Having Example 2
This example has a WHERE clause
Only movie rows in years 1990-93 are selected
The result has 10 rows.
The WHERE clause specifies which rows from the MOVIE table will beused to build the result set
SELECT		relyear, rating_code, COUNT(*)
FROM 		movie
WHERE 		relyear >= 1990
AND                relyear <= 1993
GROUP BY  	relyear, rating_code
ORDER BY	1, 2

SQL Group By Having Example 2
This example now has a HAVING clause
The having clause evaluates rows in the result set using the Aggregate Count(*) expression
The HAVING clause specifies which rows from the result set are keptor eliminated.
SELECT		relyear, rating_code, COUNT(*)
FROM 		movie
WHERE 		relyear >= 1990
AND                  relyear <= 1993
GROUP BY  	relyear, rating_code
HAVING           COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY	1, 2

SQL Typical Error Message
Most Aggregate expressions must have a matching Group By clause
Students often encounter the "Not a single-group group function error" when using aggregate expressions
If you see this error, check if you are using non-aggregate and aggregate expressions correctly
SELECT	relyear, COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
GROUP BY 	relyear
✔
SELECT	relyear, COUNT(*)
FROM 	movie
🗶

MS Access and SQL
You can use SQL in MS Access
Steps:
Create a Query by Design
Do not add any table to the query
Click the SQL icon at the bottom right of the design window
Type your SQL
Click the View icon on the menu bar to view the Query By Design window / run the query

MS Access and SQL
You can use SQL in MS Access

MS Access and SQL
A more complex example:
If you run the query and then return to the SQL window, the query will often be reformatted – usually with more brackets added to the code.

MS Access and SQL
An example with inner joins:
When you use INNER JOINS in you Access SQL, you must enclose the table names and join clauses with brackets.
Always true, if two inner joins are used
Sometimes true is one inner join is used.

MS Access and SQL
Example with four tables and three inner joins:
SELECT E.enrolmentid, S.firstname, S.lastname, C.coursename, O.orgname
FROM  ( ( (enrolment E
INNER JOIN student S            ON E.studentid = S.studentid )
INNER JOIN course C             ON E.courseid = C.courseid )
INNER JOIN organization O  ON S.orgid = O.orgid )
